subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The chapter's whole purpose is posing and solving linear equations in one unknown — from the weighing-scale and matchstick problems that yield 2n + 1 = 99 to systematically solving equations by operating equally on both sides and applying this to puzzles and word problems about parties, savings, marbles, taxis, and number tricks.

Before solving, the chapter forms algebraic expressions using variables, coefficients, and constants and manipulates them — building 2n + 1, 3k + 1, 25(f + 5), 4x − 4, and 4000 + 650m from real situations and simplifying and transposing terms — which is the forming and manipulation of algebraic expressions, a distinct strand from solving the resulting equations.

adjunct_competencies.justification: Section 7.4 is a history of algebra centred on Indian contributions — Brahmagupta's Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta (628 CE) as one of the earliest works of algebra, Aryabhata's (499 CE) systematic method, Bhāskarāchārya's Bījgaṇita (1150 CE) horse problem, the Bakhśhāli Manuscript (300 CE), and the ancient notation yā/kā/rū — an explicit appreciation of Indian mathematicians' contributions.

The 'Mind the Mistake, Mend the Mistake' section has students evaluate worked solutions, identify and justify errors, and correct them, and the generating-equations chains and the note that algebra lets us justify claims (such as why the sum of two odd numbers is even) develop convincing, logically-checked argument.

The chapter is rich in puzzles inviting students to find their own solutions — the balanced weighing-scale puzzles, Riyaz's think-of-a-number trick, digit and brick puzzles, the input-output machines, the solve-your-path maze, and the heads-and-feet children-and-donkeys problem.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to form algebraic expressions for unknown quantities and to pose and solve linear equations in one variable by performing the same operation on both sides, applying this to puzzles and daily-life word problems.
